summary: Gruber compares Google's Pixel 8 launch event to Apple's iPhone 15 event, noting they announced identical product categories — phones, watches, and earbuds — yet Google attracted far less attention and holds just 3% U.S. smartphone market share. He argues that something ineffable beyond product quality explains the Pixel's lack of traction, drawing a parallel to the Mac's long struggle to regain PC market share. The most striking announcements were AI photography features like Magic Editor and Best Take, which Gruber finds technically impressive but ethically concerning. He questions whether Google is racing toward a world where photos reflect moments that never actually happened, and whether the rest of the industry will follow.
key_points: Google and Apple announced identical product slates (phones, watches, earbuds), forming competing personal computing ecosystems that users don't mix and match
Pixel holds just 3% U.S. smartphone market share despite being Google's best market, and Google's best software being available on iOS undermines the case for switching to Pixel hardware
Google's AI photography features like Best Take cross an ethical line from capturing reality more accurately (like HDR) to fabricating moments that never occurred
The Pixel's lack of market traction has something ineffable about it that can't be explained by side-by-side product comparisons alone
Google treats its Pixel portfolio like Apple treats Apple TV hardware — making the best parts available on other platforms, reducing the perceived value of their own devices
money_quotes: There's something ineffable about it. There are aspects of marketshare traction — in any market — that can't be explained by side-by-side product comparisons alone.
The best software from Google is available on all phones; the best from Apple is only available on iPhones.
I suspect I could save this column and publish it a year from now instead of today, and barely change a word.
Pixel phone photography is racing ahead to a world where images reflect moments that never actually were.
Is the purpose of a camera to capture things as they were, or as they should have been?
Google treats its entire Pixel portfolio like Apple treats Apple TV hardware. They make the best parts of it available on other platforms, greatly reducing the perceived value of their own hardware.
3 percent market share in the U.S. — Pixel's best market — is a pretty dim 'bright spot'.
key_insight: Google's Pixel ecosystem suffers from a fundamental strategic contradiction: by making its best software available on all platforms, Google eliminates the primary reason anyone would switch to its own hardware.
